elements.sent-2411-119: Graph theory has long served as a bridge between discrete mathematics and the molecular sciences, particularly through its applications in chemical graph theory.
elements.sent-2411-120: In computational chemistry, where the objective is to model and predict molecular properties without relying directly on quantum mechanical computations, molecular graphs provide a powerful abstraction.
elements.sent-2411-121: In such representations, atoms correspond to vertices, and chemical bonds to edges, often omitting hydrogen atoms for simplicity \cite{gutman1972graph, karelson1996quantum .}
elements.sent-2411-122: Within this framework, topological indices quantitative measures derived from the structure of molecular graphs are used to correlate molecular structure with chemical, physical, and biological properties.
elements.sent-2411-123: Among the earliest and most influential topological indices are the first and second Zagreb indices, denoted $\mathcal{M _1(\mathbb{G )$ and $\mathcal{M _2(\mathbb{G )$, respectively.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-124: Introduced by Gutman and Trinajsti\'{c \cite{gutman1972graph , these indices capture degree-based connectivity patterns and are defined for a graph $\mathbb{G = (\mathcal{V , \mathcal{E )$ as follows: \begin{equation}M_1(G) = \sum_{v \in V(G)} \zeta_G(v)^2, \quad M_2(G) = \sum_{uv \in E(G)} \zeta_G(u) \zeta_G(v),\end{equation} where $\zeta_\mathbb{G (v)$ denotes the degree of vertex $v$ in $\mathbb{G}$.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-125: These indices have been widely applied in QSAR/QSPR studies for predicting molecular stability, boiling points, and reactivity \cite{todeschini2008handbook, nikolic2003zagreb .}
elements.sent-2411-126: In an effort to improve the predictive power of topological descriptors, particularly with respect to molecular branching and steric effects, Estrada et al. introduced the $ABC$ index \cite{estrada1998atom , defined as: \begin{equation}ABC(G) = \sum_{uv \in E(G)} \sqrt{\frac{\zeta_G(u) + \zeta_G(v) - 2}{\zeta_G(u)\, \zeta_G(v)}}.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-127: This index has demonstrated strong correlations with thermodynamic and structural properties, and has since become an important tool in chemical graph theory \cite{furtula2009atom .}
elements.sent-2411-128: A substantial body of work has been devoted to characterizing extremal values and establishing bounds for the $ABC$ and Zagreb indices in various graph classes.
elements.sent-2411-129: For trees, Furtula et al. \cite{furtula2009atom showed that the star graph $\mathbb{S _n$ attains the maximum $ABC$ index, a result further explored in chemical tree structures of bounded degree \cite{vassilev2012minimum .}}}
elements.sent-2411-130: Chen and Guo \cite{chen2011extreme extended $ABC$ extremal studies to catacondensed hexagonal systems and showed that edge deletion reduces the $ABC$ index.}

elements.sent-2411-266: In this paper, we investigated the influence of metric dimension on three key topological indices--the first Zagreb index, the second Zagreb index, and the $ABC$ index--within the class of trees.
elements.sent-2411-267: We established sharp upper and lower bounds for the Zagreb indices and derived an upper bound for the $ABC$ index, all in terms of the order and metric dimension of the trees.
elements.sent-2411-268: Our results revealed that path graphs attain the minimum Zagreb indices, while star graphs maximize both the Zagreb and $ABC$ indices, highlighting their extremal nature with respect to these measures.
elements.sent-2411-269: These findings provide deeper insights into how structural parameters, particularly metric dimension, affect the behavior of degree-based indices in tree graphs.
elements.sent-2411-270: Such insights have meaningful implications in chemical graph theory, where these indices serve as molecular descriptors in QSPR and QSAR models.
elements.sent-2411-271: The study contributes to the theoretical understanding of topological indices and lays the groundwork for future research into more complex graph families and the role of other structural parameters in influencing topological descriptors.
